data.title: RPM International: Self-Help Offers Hope, But More Is Needed To Support The Multiple
data.description:
For better or worse, the key question with RPM International Inc. (RPM) remains what it has long been – how much of a premium should investors pay for a company that offers above-average quality, but arguably not as quality as commonly thought. RPM has strong brands and an enviable track record of consistency, but metrics like tangible book value growth, ROIC, and FCF conversion don’t stand out as particularly special, particularly in light of a multiple that has expanded relative to its peers over the last two decades.

RPM shares are up a bit since my last update, thanks to a strong rally after fiscal third quarter earnings. Even with that pop, though, the shares have lagged the broader industrial space, as well as peers like Sherwin-Williams (SHW) and PPG Industries (PPG), while Sika (SXYAY) has continued to perform poorly.

I’m reluctant to overpay for RPM, but I do think the story is improving. RPM has shown it can generate better organic growth than many peers despite a difficult market environment, and I like the company’s ongoing pivot toward value-added “solutions” (vs. discrete products), as well as ongoing margin and capital efficiency efforts. I can argue for 10% to 15% near-term upside, but I’m still cautious about paying a high premium for a company that has lagged the S&P 500 (SP500) over the long term and not shown sustained improvement in key quality metrics.

A Strong Rebound In Fiscal Q3

While RPM did underperform in fiscal Q2, as I previewed in that earlier piece, the third quarter saw a nice rebound with real evidence of positive incremental margins and organic growth despite modest pricing activity.

Revenue rose 3% in organic terms, with pricing contributing about a point. This was good for a 4% beat versus Street expectations. The Consumer business saw about 2% organic contraction, a small beat, with a weak DIY market and ongoing product rationalizations weighing on results. The Construction segment generated about 7% organic growth, beating by about 5%, with an easy year-ago comp helping support mid-single-digit volume growth. The Performance segment beat by 10%, with 5% organic growth also driven by volume growth helped by easier comps.

Gross margin improved a point from the prior year (39.5% vs. 38.5%), but was weaker on a sequential basis (down over a point) in what is typically the seasonally weakest quarter.

Operating income rose 49%, beating by about 37%, with margin up about two points to 7.2%. Segment profits rose 32% (with margin up almost two points to 9.6%), with Consumer up 16% (beating by 7%), Construction up 273% (beating by 77%), and Performance up 20% (beating by 29%).

A Tricky Market Offset By Self-Help

The outlook for RPM isn’t really any clearer now than it was six months ago. Construction spending should continue to improve from here, but the war with Iran has added uncertainty to raw material costs, future interest rates, and business investment/expansion plans.

The actual impact of the conflict with Iran on RPM is nuanced. While RPM does rely on a variety of inputs that are, or are derived from, petrochemicals, about 90% of the company’s sales base is in North America and Europe, where supply concerns are low. That said, North American producers have been reluctant to significantly increase operating rates for fear of overbuilding inventories, and there is a risk that higher cost inflation will impact margins in the coming quarters and offset progress on self-help initiatives.

As far as end-markets go, construction is still more “recovering” than “recovered.” Activity should improve as 2026 moves on, and in the meantime I continue to expect RPM to benefit from higher-value opportunities in niches like data centers and pharma plants. Moreover, with new building activity more muted, there does appear to be a shift back to repair and maintenance (to extend the life and/or improve the efficiency of existing facilities) that should benefit the company.

The consumer market is still not particularly healthy. The DIY market remains weak, with a softening housing market and affordability issues both likely impacting demand.

Multiple Self-Help Opportunities

The macro situation is what it is, and there’s not much that the company can do to offset it. While RPM has historically enjoyed good pricing power, management seems to be taking a restrained approach for now, and that should help support volumes in the near term.

At the time, the company continues to invest in innovation. RPM has been focusing more on value-added turnkey solutions for commercial clients, and I expect new product flows to continue to help the company gain share, particularly in higher-value segments like data centers, manufacturing, and pharma.

The company is also accelerating its MAP 3.0 self-help program. Management has identified additional incremental manufacturing and procurement opportunities, and SG&A optimization has likewise become a bigger priority with management targeting $100M in improvements through the end of fiscal 2027. Efforts are also underway to reduce working capital intensity and allocate capital more efficiently, both of which could lead to longer-term improvements in FCF generation and ROIC.

The Outlook

While the third quarter was stronger than expected, guidance was somewhat tempered, with a wide EBIT range for Q4, the midpoint of which was moderately below Street expectations.

The biggest near-term risk I see revolves around the ongoing conflict with Iran. Higher energy and material prices are not only a threat to RPM’s margins (though pricing and procurement efforts can mitigate them to some extent), but also to the rate outlook and the economy in general, as higher energy prices are a known driver of weaker economic activity.

Longer term, the biggest risk I see here is multiple compression. RPM has a good record of consistent revenue and FCF growth, and operating/EBITDA margins have been stronger of late, but the company’s track record with FCF generation and ROIC are more mixed. I do think another three points of margin improvement are possible over the next decade, but if RPM underperforms here I do think there is a real risk that RPM’s above-average multiples will revert back toward the mean.

I’m looking for mid-single-digit growth across the next three years and over the longer term as well, with the latter growth rate around 4%. I expect the company’s margin and capital efficiency efforts to pay off with high-teens EBITDA margins and low double-digit FCF margins over time, driving around 8% FCF growth.

RPM is still pricy on discounted cash flow, suggesting even higher expectations on the Street and/or a willingness to accept an even lower discount rate as a reward for RPM’s quality. Multiples-based approaches (EV/EBITDA, P/B, et al) driven by margins, ROIC, ROE, et al are more favorable, and I can support a fair value around $120 on that basis. That still embeds a roughly 3.5x premium on EBITDA, though, so again I note the risk of multiple compression if RPM disappoints.

The Bottom Line

I’m inclined to upgrade RPM on an improved likelihood of near-term outperformance. I can argue that the shares are 10%-15% undervalued now, and I think RPM is better-placed than its peers to continue to generate organic growth despite a challenging and volatile macro backdrop, to say nothing of being well-placed for a reacceleration in the non-residential construction market in late 2026/2027.

The “but” is that I do see risk of ongoing multiple compression and I am trying to weigh the probabilities of near-term outperformance with a little more skepticism that that outperformance will persist over the long term. I’m not looking to buy RPM shares now, but I do see an argument for how the shares could outperform in 2026.

data.title: EWY: The AI Memory Supercycle Makes South Korea The Most Mispriced Market Globally
data.description:
Introduction

Previously, I’ve written a Seeking Alpha article on Micron Technology, Inc. (MU). My thesis on MU is largely based on the intense requirements of AI infrastructure creating an extraordinary level of demand for high-performance memory, which typically is a cyclical industry; however, my view is that memory companies are moving past historical cyclicality into a phase with more structural demand. The beneficiaries of this memory shortage extend beyond MU to Korean companies like SK Hynix Inc. and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF).

I am initiating coverage on the iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) with a Strong Buy rating. My core thesis hinges on an AI memory supercycle where demand is now structural & not cyclical, an overhaul of Korean companies through the Value-Up program set to decrease the “Korea discount,” and a macroeconomic recovery that is decoupling from traditional emerging market risks.

A Concentrated Play On Memory

The traditional understanding of the memory industry is that it is bound to the cyclical fluctuations of consumer demand. However, this cyclicality is being replaced with structural demand driven by the expansion of AI infrastructure. The driver of this memory supercycle is High Bandwidth Memory (HBM). As LLMs grow in complexity, the bottleneck for AI training and inference has moved on from being raw compute power to memory bandwidth.

HBM4 requires approximately three times the wafer capacity of standard DRAM to produce the same number of bits. This increased die size significantly reduces the total number of chips available for the broader market, creating a structural undersupply in traditional memory used for PCs and mobile devices. As shown in the chart above, bandwidth and transfer speed per generation are increasing, showing that memory requirements for newer GPU generations are increasing & consequently there is more demand for memory.

According to Goldman Sachs, CapEx estimates for AI hyperscalers have risen to $527 billion in 2026 following guidance from Q3 earnings. Much of this CapEx is expected to go towards GPUs, and 66% of the cost of GPUs is represented by memory.

For investors seeking to capitalize on this supercycle, EWY provides concentrated exposure to two of the three biggest memory manufacturers: SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics. While many market-cap-weighted S&P 500 funds are often criticized for heavily weighting the Magnificent 7, the cumulative 43.5% weighting of these two memory companies within EWY is a feature and not a detracting factor in my opinion. This heavy concentration is what has allowed EWY to capture the outsized gains seen within memory manufacturers.

The high pricing increases seen in NAND/DRAM have led to a significant positive impact on EWY’s NAV. Furthermore, these price increases are not expected to slow down any time soon. TrendForce projects DRAM contract prices to increase 58% to 63% quarter-over-quarter in Q2 2026. This is essentially a further boost to the top line of memory manufacturers like Samsung and SK Hynix, and it is important to note that these pricing increases are also expanding margins, partially offsetting any additional CapEx going towards expanding capacity.

Dismantling The Korea Discount

The Korea Composite Stock Price Index trades at a forward P/E ratio of 8.8x, which seems incredibly low when compared to the S&P 500, which trades at a much higher forward P/E ratio of 21.2x. That is a 140% gap between the P/E ratios of the two indexes. Furthermore, Macquarie projects a 48% EPS growth in 2026, while the S&P 500 2026 EPS growth projection is just 14%. I believe that EWY’s holdings are going to be subject to both multiple expansion and earnings growth.

For many years, South Korean companies traded at lower valuations than their global peers due to poor corporate governance, low shareholder returns, and generally opaque family-controlled management structures. SK Hynix, for example, trades at a 4.92x forward P/E ratio, whereas MU trades at a 6.3x forward P/E ratio. I believe that this discount is being narrowed by a series of reforms known as the "Value-Up" program.

In July 2025, the government amended the Commercial Act to introduce a director's fiduciary duty to all shareholders. The goal of this amendment to the Commercial Act is to protect shareholders from actions by controlling families, such as unfair mergers or the hoarding of cash. Furthermore, the Korean National Assembly approved dividend tax reforms, reducing the tax rate on dividend income to a range of 14% to 30%.

The average total payout ratio of the Korean banking sector is expected to increase to more than 50% in 2026 from 36% in 2023. This shows that Korean companies are engaging in more shareholder-friendly behavior by optimizing their capital allocation to holding only the capital they need & returning excess capital to shareholders instead of letting it sit idle.

The South Korean Economy

The South Korean economy is skewed toward high-tech exports - in 2025, total exports from South Korea exceeded $700 billion for the first time. The KOSPI index is historically closely correlated with export performance, often leading export trends by approximately two months. The current strength in semiconductor ASPs and volume shipments suggests an excellent earnings outlook for 2026.

The "K-shaped" recovery mentioned by the Bank of Korea (BoK) highlights the widening disparity between the strong IT sector and the struggling non-IT sectors, such as traditional construction. For EWY, this is a favorable dynamic, as the fund is heavily weighted toward the outperformers.

From a monetary & fiscal policy perspective, the Bank of Korea has adopted a neutral viewpoint with the policy rate at 2.5%. Inflation slightly deviates from the 2.0% target, with the previously expected rate set to be at around 2.2%. BoK has indicated that it will focus on economic growth, not rate hikes.

EWY Construction

EWY tracks the MSCI Korea 25/50 Index. No single company can exceed 25% of the index weight. Additionally, the sum of all group entities with a weight greater than 5% is limited to 50% of the index. The index is rebalanced on a quarterly basis.

Above are the top 10 holdings in EWY. As previously mentioned, collectively Samsung and SK Hynix represent a significant portion of EWY at a 43.5% weightage. Beyond semiconductors, EWY also has significant automotive presence from Hyundai Motor and KIA Corporation. While automotive companies generally haven’t provided outsized returns, Hyundai, in the past year alone, has appreciated over 180%. Much of this share price appreciation can be attributed to a physical AI/robotics and autonomous driving technologies story. KB Financial & Shinhan, for example, are re-rating due to the Value-Up initiative & its effects on the banking sector as previously discussed.

Above are some basic metrics on EWY. EWY’s expense ratio of 0.59% is quite high, but given that most of the equities contained within EWY are not readily accessible in Western markets & these equities are very high quality in my opinion, I think it is reasonable to pay such an expense ratio. EWY has total net assets of $16.6 billion, and so I believe there are no significant liquidity issues with the fund.

Risks

South Korea’s high reliance on exports is vulnerable to global trade tensions. The potential escalation of US tariff measures could weigh on the outlook for automobiles and steel. Additionally, given semiconductors are viewed as a domestically critical sector in the US, there could be higher levels of competitive pressure arising due to governmental support to companies like Micron from regulations like the Chips & Science Act.

There are concerns regarding over-investment in AI. If the value generated from the adoption of AI tools ends up being disproportional to the high costs of the infrastructure buildout, it could be the case that hyperscalers cut CapEx spending. This cut in spending will lead to significant drawdowns for memory stocks, which EWY is highly concentrated in.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe that EWY presents a very compelling investment opportunity for investors looking for exposure to high-quality memory producers. EWY presents exposure to the South Korean economy in the midst of significant corporate restructuring aimed at decreasing the Korean valuation discount to global companies. Additionally, I believe that while there are risks of over-investment into AI, potential trade tensions, and inflationary effects from high oil prices, I also think there is very high share price upside driven by the high concentration of memory producers.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Options Become Lucrative Amid Unusual Volatilities
data.description:
XOM stock: FQ1 earnings meets Middle East war

I last covered Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) on February 24 with an article titled “Exxon Mobil: Prepare For A Potential $90 Oil Price Scenario.” The article reviewed the company’s FQ4 earnings report (ER) and explained the key catalysts that could push oil prices much higher (and oil prices were around $66 at that time). Since then, a few new developments have emerged around XOM and largely obsoleted my earlier thesis. To start, the war with Iran has pushed oil prices—currently hovering around $99 as of this writing—to be above my projections already. Secondly, the war in the Middle East has also materially impacted XOM’s production capacity by up to 6%, as detailed in the following news release. Such a notable change in production, which is one of the parameters in my previous analysis, also calls for a reassessment. Third, the company is also scheduled to release its FQ1 2026 earnings report (ER) on April 10.

Seeking Alpha News: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) said Wednesday that disruptions to its assets in Qatar and the United Arab Emirates will reduce its global oil-equivalent production by 6% in Q1 compared to the previous quarter, as the Iran war paralyzed much of the Persian Gulf energy industry. Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) said its upstream assets in Qatar and the United Arab Emirates, which account for ~20% of the company's global oil-equivalent barrels of production, were impacted by production disruptions beginning in early March.

Finally, as a response to the above developments, XOM shares have demonstrated a significant volatility increase since my last writing, both in terms of their HV (historical volatility) and also IV (implied volatility) in the option market. Actually, the volatilities behind these shares have reached unusual levels in my model. For example, the next chart shows the 30-day rolling HV for Exxon, illustrating a significant surge in the past few days. Its HV started around 17% in the beginning of this year, and this metric has surged sharply to roughly 30% as of this writing, among the highest levels YTD. In terms of IV, the reading is among the top 8% percentile rank in the past year (more on this in a minute).

Next, I will further explore the implications of such unusual levels of volatiles and explain why I consider them as overestimates for the stock’s true uncertainties.

XOM stock: Intraday price fluctuations are elevated, too

The rest of the discussion needs a bit of technical background behind the concepts of IV and HV. For readers unfamiliar with them yet, they are important parameters that many traders use to estimate the expected price change of a given security. My earlier article provides a detailed tutorial of how the math works; a brief recap is quoted below for ease of reference:

The stock price changes, under certain assumptions, follow the so-called “Square Root of Time” rule. The rule simply states that the price change scales with the STD (standard deviation) of the price changes and also the square root of time (hence the name). The rule is based on the assumption that stock price movements behavior as a random walker with step sizes following a normal distribution. It is beyond the scope of this article to debate the accuracy of this assumption, and readers interested in this topic could take a look at Benoit Mandelbrot’s book titled “The Misbehavior of Markets: A Fractal View of Financial Turbulence”.

With the above background, the next chart shows the interday price changes for XOM (i.e., the day-to-day close price changes). As seen, in the past 20 trading days, the price change averaged about 1.62% and exhibited an STD of 2.14%. If you multiply 2.14% by the square root of 251 (the average trading days in a year), you will get 33.8%, which is often quoted as the 20-day rolling HV. It is an important parameter as it helps investors to estimate the expected range of stock price movement in a year (which is about 33.8% in either direction in this case).

Another volatility metric I like to monitor is the intraday price fluctuations. As an example, the next chart shows my calculation of the price change of XOM between a trading day’s price peak and bottom. My main takeaway here is that the intraday fluctuations have become even more pronounced than the above interday changes. To wit, the intraday fluctuations averaged 3.12% with an STD of 4.13% in the past 20 days, both almost doubling the readings for the interday fluctuations quoted above. When intraday volatility is larger than the daily close price change, experiences have taught me that the market is going through a high degree of indecision and increased churn.

XOM stock: Elevated volatility provides an attractive premium for option sellers

The above issues have implied an unusually wide range of XOM share prices in the near term. As an example, the next table shows how the potential price range in 1 month (calendar month) follows the square-root-time rule. Based on a current price of $156 and a 20-day inter-day STD of 2.14%, XOM has an implied percentage move of 11.3% in a month, creating an implied price range between $138 and $174. Because the share’s intraday volatilities are even higher, as aforementioned, they imply even wider price changes during intraday trading. To wit, based on the share’s current price and a 20-day intraday STD of 4.13%, XOM has an implied percentage move of 21.8% in a month, creating an implied price range between $122 and $190.

As another data point to illustrate the volatilities, the screenshot below (taken from MarketChameleon) shows the IV of XOM on the option market. As seen, XOM’s IV currently hovers around 32.3%, far above its 52-week historical average of 24.2% by more than 33%. In terms of percentile, XOM’s current IV puts it in the 92nd percentile rank, which means the current level of IV was only observed 8% of the time in the past year. Finally, note that the 20-day HV quoted here is 33.9%, quite close to what I computed above following the square-root-time rule (33.8%).

Due to the unusual level of HV and IV, XOM’s current option premiums are favorable for sellers in my experience. As a specific example, let’s take a closer look at its option chain data provided by Seeking Alpha below. An NTM (near-the-money) put option with an expiration date of May 15 with a $155 strike price last traded at $6.35. This is more than 4% of XOM’s current share price, hence offering investors a sizable income and/or a hedge against downward price movement. Also note that these NTM options all have sizable trading volume and also open interest (both in the thousands for the $155 strike example), which leads to plenty of liquidity and relatively narrow bid-ask spreads, as seen.

XOM stock: other risks and final thoughts

The above analysis also applies to the investors who want to sell call options. It is just that I am a bit more inclined toward downward hedging under current conditions. There are a few reasons behind my caution here. First, also mentioned upfront, oil prices have already reached my near-term target. Second, XOM shares are also currently trading at a sizable valuation premium either by horizontal or vertical comparison. As seen in the chart below, the stock’s P/E (on a non-GAAP and TTM basis) is 22.35, which is more than 35% above the sector median and 72% above its own five-year average of 12.99.

The valuation premium is also reflected in its below-average dividend yield. As seen in the next chart, Exxon Mobil currently offers a dividend yield of 2.59%, which is not only notably below its long-term average of 4.37% but also near the lowest levels in the past 10 years. Finally, for readers new to the use of options, a strong word of caution is in order. The last thing I want from my above discussion is to mislead you into the impression that option sellers win either way. The risk calculus for an option selling must be understood from the angle of opportunity cost. For example, in the case of selling put options (what I discussed above), the sellers are contractually bound to sell their shares at the strike price regardless of the share market at execution. As such, they could face unlimited downside risks, as the share price could, in theory, rise to infinity.

All told, this XOM revisit is triggered mainly by 3 new catalysts since my last writing: the war in the Middle East, XOM’s upcoming FQ1 2026 earnings report, and the changes in XOM’s price volatilities. These issues, when coupled with XOM’s current valuation and production uncertainties, have caused me to take a cautious stance for long-term commitment. In the meantime, the unusually higher volatilities provide an interesting setup for investors interested in options in the near term.

data.title: Everspin Technologies: A Little Expensive For What It Is
data.description:
As the share price of Everspin Technologies, Inc. (MRAM) recovered from its sub-$5 lows and now that we have its full-year 2025 performance, I wanted to revisit it to see if it would be a good time to initiate a position. Unfortunately, it seems a little expensive given the lack of profitability improvements and lackluster top-line growth. I missed the boat at around $5 a share, and right now, the risk-to-reward is not enticing.

Briefly on Performance

Looking at the company’s top-line performance for 2025, we can see a steady increase in sales, mostly helped by product sales. On average, the company’s top line grew around 10%. It would have been higher, but the lumpy license revenue brought it down slightly, because this segment saw a 15% decline for the year. The demand for the product is still there, I see, but it is not as strong as I wanted it in the past before. Nevertheless, the company had 238 design wins this past year, up from 178 in the prior year.

Going over the company’s profitability, we can see that every metric across the board weakened slightly. This makes sense to me, because the company’s royalty and licensing revenues took a hit at the end of the year, and we know that these are the main drivers for higher margins. Licensing and royalty revenues are higher because they do not carry the same manufacturing and logistics costs as making an actual product. At least the decline wasn’t that big. I expect to see this improving with more demand for the IP and products in general. Overall, though, the company is still not profitable, and that could be worrisome if the company is not prepared to weather the downturns.

Speaking of weathering downturns, let’s take a look at how well it is prepared in terms of the company’s financial position. MRAM finished the year with around $44.5m in cash and equivalents against no debt on its books. So, in short, it is well prepared to continue to operate at a slight loss as it does right now.

The cash on hand makes a decent interest income for MRAM, which actually pushed it to profitability in Q4 ’25. Its cash from operations was solid for the year as well, close to $10m; however, because the company spent more than double on CapEx in 2025, its free cash flow took a slight hit, going from $4m to $3.1m.

Overall, the company seems to continue to chug along as a niche memory player. It is not growing as fast as some of the main players in the memory space, like Micron (MU) or SanDisk (SNDK), but there is a reason why that is, and I will delve deeper into this in the next section.

Is MRAM here to replace DRAM and NAND?

The management said in the past that the near-term growth story is centered on data center, energy management, and industrial automation demand, with design wins expected to see a ramp over the next couple of years. You might think that this is akin to the rise in memory demand for all things AI data centers; however, I don’t think we are going to see such a surge in demand for MRAM technology here. The technology of MRAM is not here to replace DRAM. Mainly because MRAM should be thought of as a specialty memory that can replace some DRAM-based or SRAM-based designs where persistence, low power, and high endurance matter. However, what MRAM isn’t and probably won’t ever be is that it is not dense enough or even cheap enough to take over mainstream DRAM capacity roles. Hyperscalers are looking for scale. You are not getting scale from MRAM. It can replace persistent DRAM solutions, but not everything across the board. The technology is not meant to be the bulk main memory.

MRAM technology is non-volatile, which means it keeps data without power, which makes it useful where batteries or supercapacitors would be needed otherwise to back up DRAM and SRAM. It is also as durable as DRAM, and even much better than NAND flash, which is why MRAM shows up in SSD controllers, industrial systems, and transaction-log style workloads.

You may ask why DRAM and NAND will continue to dominate, and that is because Micron’s business is built around high-volume, dense memory DRAM and NAND. These markets are heavily driven by cost per bit and massive scale. MRAM is just not built for that, at least not yet, to be able to handle such massive manufacturing scale and offer the best unit economics. Right now, most computing systems care about scale and costs rather than persistence or endurance. These enterprises can replicate what MRAM does with batteries and supercapacitors to get persistence.

So, even if we think about the total costs, which MRAM argues the technology ends up being competitive or even cheaper, but only in some persistent-memory designs. This is a very narrow use case, and not a commodity memory replacement. Besides, since DRAM and NAND are so dominant right now in the data center space, these memory players already have their own architecture that allows for persistence, like the CXL-attached DRAM-based memory expansion. The whole ecosystem of staying within DRAM is appealing for data centers, so there is no need to switch to MRAM, at least right now.

So, in short, the technology is not even trying to replace DRAM and NAND because they are not targeting the same needs from data centers. It is targeting the support memory, write buffers, configuration storage, and some persistent-memory layers, where instant retention and fast writes matter more than sheer density.

How should we think about MRAM?

So, if the case is not to replace DRAM at hyperscale, we should think of it, as the management puts it, as “memory around compute.” That way, we can see that it is not trying to become the compute memory itself. For this niche, the TAM opportunity is only a small slice of the data center stack.

Is there any reason to think the company will see a substantial increase in product manufacturing going forward? MRAM technologies seem to think so. The company recently expanded on-shore MRAM manufacturing capacity with Microchip (MCHP). This means that MCHP will allow MRAM to use its fabrication capacity in Oregon so that Everspin can make MRAM and TMR sensor products there. The deal is for 10 years initially and extendable in 2-year increments. Clearly, the company is getting ready for an influx of demand, or it is trying to avoid capacity constraints ahead of the demand increase, so they are ready when it does increase. The company expected to see a proper ramp in 2027 and 2028.

I don’t think we are going to get a similar situation as we saw with other memory players I mentioned earlier, whose capacity constraints led to a massive surge in prices, which trickled down straight into their bottom lines. But who knows?

Closing Comments

If we are going to see a surge in demand for 2027 and 2028, I would like to see the company’s operations improve dramatically. The increase in demand should help its operational leverage, meaning its fixed costs should be spread out more efficiently across a lot more units. The utilization rates should be close to 100%, and so I would expect to see the company hitting profitability a lot more consistently over the next couple of years.

Am I looking to start a position? Unfortunately, when my price alert was triggered at $7 a share, I didn’t initiate position, and completely skipped my mind as I was focused on other companies, so right now, I am going to pass on it for now, and wait to see how the company’s profitability progresses over the next few quarters.

data.title: ECARX Holdings: DreamSmart Is A Bold Move, Not A Free One
data.description:
Thesis

Founded in 2017 with roots in Shanghai, China, ECARX Holdings Inc. (ECX) is a small-cap global tech company that primarily serves automotive OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers. ECARX specializes in creating "smart" vehicles, which include features such as large touchscreen displays, voice assistants, navigation systems, and additional applications that allow vehicles to function like smartphones on wheels. ECARX has a significant international presence and is expanding its operations throughout Europe, Asia, and North America.

Yesterday, ECX hit my radar after going up 14%. Let’s not get too excited though, since this was just recently a penny stock, so that’s only $0.14. Nevertheless, being intrigued by the opportunity to get in “early” on a high-risk small-cap stock, which could generally mean amplified gains, and after going over a one-two punch of recent news via an acquisition plan and an audit of its 2025 full-year results, I’m inclined to buy the shares because the stock seems priced lower than what the company’s growth potential suggests. Its profit margins are getting better, spending is becoming more controlled, and its software plan could add significant upside if the company executes it well.

ECARX Holdings: Improving, but Not Easy

  • Revenue (2025): $847.9M (+10% YoY)
  • Sales of Goods Revenue (Q4): $270M (+27% YoY)
  • Services Revenue (Q4): $33M
  • Software License Revenue (Q4): $2M
  • Advanced platforms share of goods revenue: 74%.

The earnings report tells us that revenue growth predominantly came from the product business, particularly computing platforms, while service and software licensing were weaker; therefore it appears that we continue to see stronger product-market fit, but this isn't the only factor influencing ECX's results because hardware shipments are still largely dependent on their scheduled delivery and an improvement in product mix toward higher value devices. Basically, the usual "project timing" reasoning we often hear in these conference calls. The general takeaway is that these businesses continue to have a heavy dependency on launch cadence, customer program timing, and physical realities associated with automotive manufacturing.

  • Gross Margin (2025): 19%
  • Q4 Gross Margin: 21%
  • Gross Profit (2025): $161.3M
  • Q4 Gross Profit: $64M
  • Q4 Net Income: $2.8M
  • Q4 Adjusted EBITDA: ~$22M
  • 2026 Gross Margin Outlook: 15–18%.

It seems that margins have improved for legitimate operational reasons rather than cosmetic ones. The management team cited a quartet of improvements from the product mix, more vertically integrated operations, more disciplined cost controls and improved execution on design-to-value processes to support these improvements. For context, the quarter was impacted by the ongoing cost pressures from components, especially memory, which management called out multiple times. Rising costs, as you know, are a real issue across the wider market. The memory market has been under pressure because demand from AI has increased a lot. This, in turn, has led to big increases in contract prices, and supply is expected to remain limited for the foreseeable future.

Yes, ECARX deserves a brief standing ovation for defending its margins through pricing actions, customer collaboration, and cost engineering. That points to real operational improvement. At the same time, this means the current level of profit margins shouldn’t be assumed to stay the same. If the costs of materials or inputs remain high, or if OEM customers push back more strongly against price increases, then those margins could come under pressure again.

  • Operating Expenses (Q4): $57M (down 19% YoY)
  • Operating Expenses (FY): $216M ( down 24% YoY)
  • SG&A (FY): down 14% YoY
  • R&D (Q4): down 39% YoY.

While gross profit is important, the bigger picture is expense management. The audited results and the call tell us that lower operating expenses were achieved through disciplined selling & marketing and administrative costs plus a leaner more efficient R&D process. The management even attributed some of this to the increased use of AI tools, which may be valid at the margin, but I think over-romanticizing it is a stretch. The major point is that ECARX appears to be standardizing platforms and reusing engineering work across customers, which is precisely why the company will be able to grow this model.

Even so, there is an inherent tension in that the company wants to maintain a "lean" approach while, like most small companies, they have to invest in expanding outside of its domestic market, performing additional work associated with obtaining certifications, establishing a Germany hub and developing next generation "AI cockpit" and cross-domain products. This can work only if they can convert commercial wins within a reasonable period of time.

Now onto the acquisition plan. I think the reason why the DreamSmart deal makes strategic sense should be pretty obvious. ECARX will play a role in the overall development of FlyMe OS, and a minority stake and associated IP would allow this company to gain more influence over a software component, which clearly provides a competitive in-cabin experience.

That being said, the announcement itself does seem somewhat, let's call it, cautious, as the deal is both pending the usual due diligence and final agreement on documentation financing, regulatory requirements, and board approval. Management has also left the door open regarding how they may structure the deal, including cash, equity, or new debt, so I really can't factor those in at this point. This tells us that strategically this is reasonable, but financially there's a high level of risk.

If the acquisition strengthens the software ownership position, this could enhance bargaining power and improve platform coherence in the long run. Conversely, if financing for the acquisition comes with additional dilution, not always a good thing with companies of this size, or leverage at a time that doesn't create balance sheet strength, then the market might see this as ECARX as trying to be too ambitious before their existing balance sheet has fully earned the right.

  • 2026 Revenue Guidance: $1.0B–$1.1B (+20–30% YoY)
  • Auto market decline estimate (Q1): ~20% YoY
  • Overseas Revenue Share: 28%
  • Overseas revenue target (2030): ~50%.

To get the bigger picture, Reuters recently reported that car sales within China continued to decline in March. This happened because special discounts and incentives were reduced, while dealers were still facing weak customer demand and too much unsold inventory. At the same time, car exports from China remained strong. This matches well with the management commentary on the quarter being seasonally soft and affected by weak end market sales.

Additionally, this trend illustrates why ECARX is so vigorously pursuing global original equipment manufacturing, especially with Volkswagen (VWAGY) in Latin America, where its integration of cockpit stack and Google (GOOG) can easily transfer across brands and trims instead of just a China solution. This approach is an appropriate strategy to follow; however, executing this plan will now be the key challenge: transition the pipeline into Europe and other international markets, maintain price discipline in the current tight hardware cost environment, and continually shift towards higher quality platforms and software without abandoning the lean operating model that management states is the foundation of profitability for the remainder of the year.

ECX Stock: Cheap, But Conditional

I’d focus on future (forward) earnings and sales rather than past results, because past profits have been inconsistent and aren’t reliable enough to base a valuation on. Looking at it this way, ECX doesn’t seem expensive at first glance.

The forward P/E ratio is below average for its industry and all the forward sales multiples have very large discounts.

That makes sense given the strong revenue growth, which should improve at an even faster rate in the next couple of years, but still relatively weak profitability profile.

A low gross margin, negative trailing EBIT and EBITDA margins, and negative free cash flow may just keep the market from rewarding ECX’s growth until the company can prove said growth translates into consistent cash earnings.

So this valuation only makes sense if the company’s earnings really do grow as expected, that’s the key assumption.

Current forecasts suggest EPS will increase quickly over the next two years. However, the company still has negative operating cash flow, low cash per share, and a business that depends on factors, as I pointed out in the beginning, like when customers place orders, the mix of hardware it sells, and rising component costs. Because of all this, there’s not a lot of real buffer for bad news.

You also have to consider the risk that the company could issue more shares to raise money or fund acquisitions, which would dilute existing shareholders. Because of that, at this price, the company does need to start generating cash on a consistent basis.

Final Takeaway

Buy. I think there's enough reasonable upside potential from low valuation (compared to peers), 20-30% Y/Y revenue growth forecast, better margins, and potential additional upside from software/ platform. You still need to consider the downside risks for this small-cap because of negative cash flow, component inflation, cyclical OEM timing, and possible acquisition-driven dilution. Target buyers should be very aggressive, high-risk investors who are willing to bear some volatility and have patience until cash-generating operations become materially present. I'm going to start with a small ECX position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Strong Sell Still: Tesla's Decline Appears Far From Over
data.description:
2026 is really shaping up to be a difficult year for shareholders of electric vehicle giant Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). On April 2nd of this year, the stock dropped 5.4%. This came after management announced deliveries for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year that fell short of analysts’ expectations.

But this is just the start of the troubles that the company is facing. Since I last reaffirmed it as a Strong Sell back in February of this year, the stock has fallen 12%. The S&P 500 (SP500), meanwhile, is down just 4.8% over that window of time. And year to date, shares are down 19.8%. This is, quite honestly, abysmal. What makes matters worse is that this is already coming off of a difficult 2025. Last year, shares were up just 11.4%. But the S&P 500 rose 17.9%.

Those who are bullish about the company point to different opportunities, most recently the robotics revolution. As I detailed in my previous article about the company, that is unlikely to save it. The fact of the matter is that the firm still relies heavily on electric vehicles, and that is unlikely to change. Even under rather rosy assumptions, I don't see a scenario where the business is anything other than absurdly overvalued. Add on top of this increasing competitive pressures that have admittedly eased up in recent months as other car companies cut back on their own electric vehicle programs, weak consumer confidence, and the absence now of the $7,500 electric vehicle tax credit that expired in September of last year, and I don't see anywhere but down for the stock from this point on.

It is always possible that my opinion on the matter could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, after the market closes on April 22nd, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Overall financial performance is forecasted to improve. But I don't think it will improve enough to justify anything other than a Strong Sell rating.

The Picture Is Unlikely To Get Better

Fundamentally speaking, the last few years have been a difficult time for shareholders of Tesla. In the chart above, for instance, you can see financial results for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years, as well as results for the final quarter of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows have declined during this time. Some of this weakness can be chalked up to a drop in automotive regulatory credits that the company was receiving. In 2025, for instance, the firm brought in just $1.99 billion from those. But the year before that, they amounted to $2.76 billion. These credits are basically all profit for the company. But the issue has been beyond just that. From 2023 through 2025, the number of vehicles delivered by the company dropped. In previous articles, I had discussed how market share for the company had dropped as new models came out.

The decline in profits for the company has been particularly problematic. Margins for the firm have declined. The chart below shows, for instance, this overall contraction in profitability in relation to sales. There have been some bright spots. The Energy Generation & Storage segment, for instance, has achieved remarkable growth while simultaneously capturing strong profit margins. But this seems to be more of a side business in management's eyes than it is the part of operations that they should probably be focused most on.

The newest data provided by management has been something of a mixed bag. We actually are seeing some improvement when it comes to not only production but deliveries as well. Data released the other day by management showed that deliveries in the first quarter of 2026 ended up being 358,023 units. That was up nicely from the 336,681 reported a year earlier. This came as production grew from 362,615 to 408,386. However, even that was disappointing since analysts were expecting deliveries of 365,000 units. Keep in mind that, after a couple of years of declining market share, the company actually did see a strong final quarter of last year, with a market share of 58.9% in the US far surpassing the 40.9% that it reported a quarter earlier and the 44.4% that the firm had in the final quarter of 2024. But this had less to do with strong results from the company and more to do with the fact that other firms saw electric vehicle sales fall more than what it experienced.

At the end of September of 2025, we saw the expiration of the $7,500 electric vehicle tax credit. Leading up to that point, consumers rushed to buy new vehicles. So it stands to reason that, after that initial surge, there would be a plunge in the number of vehicles sold in the final quarter. Even though Tesla did see its market share rise during this time, the number of vehicles that it sold dropped to 138,000 compared to the 154,900 that it sold a year earlier. So weakness continues, just not to the extent that other electric vehicle companies endured.

As I have detailed in other articles, market conditions are rapidly deteriorating. As the chart above illustrates, auto loan delinquency rates are on the rise. In the final quarter of last year, they hit 5.2%. This is the highest reading that we have had since the final quarter of 2010. Even though interest rates are expected to fall from this point on, when you add on top of this the deleterious impact of tariffs, plus now high oil prices caused by the war against Iran, it's not unthinkable that the economy will head into a recession. In fact, I think it is almost guaranteed. While some might argue that high oil prices (CO1:COM) will be a boon for the electric vehicle space, I view that as more of a short-term bump, if anything. Long term, oil prices will decline, creating more challenges for the electric vehicle market even though, 10 or 20 years from now, they will be the dominant form of transportation.

On March 26th, the management team at Tesla provided data showing consensus estimates of sell-side analysts when it comes to not only the number of deliveries of vehicles that the company should experience over the next several years but also total energy storage deployments. This gave me some inspiration to create an updated model regarding what the picture of the business might look like if these estimates turn out to be accurate. To be honest with you, I did have to make a great many assumptions in this, mostly centered around margins and the rise of the firm's robotics business. I won't bore you with the details of the margin estimates. But with the exception of margins for the electric vehicles that the company is producing, I was rather generous, pushing up margins for the firm over time.

The bigger question comes down to what its robotics business, known as Optimus, will ultimately amount to. Earlier this year, Elon Musk said that the company plans to produce 1 million humanoid robots annually in the not-too-distant future. The goal here is to capture this kind of production within five years. We obviously don't know the ramp up here. And there's also significant uncertainty regarding the company's ability to even produce and sell these robots at scale at prices that would be reasonable to consumers. One source, for instance, estimated that humanoid robots will start off at around $200,000 each, eventually falling to $50,000 apiece by the year 2050. However, Elon Musk has asserted that his goal is to sell these for between $20,000 and $30,000 each. We shouldn't see any production that is sold to the public before the end of next year. So in my analysis, I assumed 250,000 units in 2028, 750,000 in 2029, and 1 million in 2030. I assumed that prices will start at $30,000 apiece and scale down to $20,000 after three years. And as scale occurs, I assume gross profit margins will grow from 20% to 30%. Considering the track record that the company has with other big promises like its severely lagging robotaxi business, I believe that these assumptions are actually quite generous to the company.

Another big assumption that I had to make was in relation to what the average price would be for each of its electric vehicles. Management is pushing to get prices down considerably. Prices of $30,000 per vehicle or less have been floated. Last year, the weighted average price was $40,230 per vehicle. So in the three tables below, I looked at three different scenarios. The first table assumes that revenue per vehicle will amount to $30,000 over the next few years. The second table puts this at up to $35,000, while the final table puts it at $40,000.

Unfortunately, even under the most optimistic assumptions that I provided here, we are looking at a business that, based on the data provided, looks horribly expensive. In the table below, you can see certain trading multiples for the company compared to how the stock is priced right now. These are incredibly high multiples, especially for a firm that has, up until at least this upcoming quarterly release, demonstrated nothing but contraction year over year for the last few years.

Of course, the picture could change as new data becomes available. I mentioned at the start of this article that management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the firm's 2026 fiscal year after the market closes on April 22nd. In the table below, you can see what the business achieved during the same time in 2025 and what analysts anticipate for the first quarter of this year. The good news is that revenue, profits, and cash flows are expected to rise. But that's already baked into my growth model for the firm. I don't really see any reason why numbers would be so radically different so as to justify anything other than a very bearish assessment.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, I would argue that Tesla might be an interesting firm, but it's not one that I think is worth investing in at this time. I would actually argue that the company is drastically overpriced, and I suspect that further weakness in its share price will occur throughout this year. In addition to severe structural and leadership-related issues, the company is horrifyingly expensive. Because of this, I believe that maintaining it as a Strong Sell is the right move.

data.title: Oscar Health: A High-Growth Story With Significant Re-Rating Potential
data.description:
Surely enough, my bullish thesis hasn't played out yet. The stock underperformed the benchmark since my last article, but I am not too worried. Quite frankly, if anything, I see tailwinds developing favorably.

I'd also like to say that my timing wasn't terrible. It's just that the stock couldn't maintain its positive momentum. All the rallies were sold off.

But what I still love about the company is that Oscar Health (OSCR) is a high-growth name, which offers a defensive approach. Obviously there are risks to consider, but recently, management guided for significant revenue growth. Well, if that were to play out, Oscar Health would trade at about a 10x earnings multiple versus the FY2027 EPS estimate of $1.35. I don't think that's expensive.

So, my rating remains unchanged. I still view it as a "Buy." Here's why.

The CEO Bought The Stock

You know there's a saying that management may sell for many reasons, but they buy for one. I also would love to point out that actions speak louder than words. So, seeing OSCR's CEO buying $12 million of the stock, well, that definitely raises confidence.

Personally, I think this purchase was at the right time. It's been a while since anyone from management bought the stock. But not only that. Oscar Health is down 33% over the past 6 months. Could this ignite a rally? Well, the stock surged 13% over the following trading day after the news. But in my honest opinion, it needs to meet the guidance to break the downtrend.

High Expectations, But Can It Materialize?

Now, what I think could definitely move the needle for the stock is accelerated revenue growth. Gladly, that's what management expects. Quite honestly, they have surprised me with a significant, high-growth guidance for 2026. Could it come to fruition? I think that's possible.

What I'd love to point out is that they have been a high-growth name for a while now. Last year they achieved strong top-line growth. But I honestly think that meaningful drop in SG&A expense ratio from 19.1% to 17.5% really makes a difference for its valuation. Basically, if they can continue to reduce costs, this may result in margin improvement. That's something that the market loves. And that eventually could result in bottom-line growth. Management thinks they could achieve a 16% SG&A expense ratio at the mid-point for 2026. Quite honestly, if they leverage AI, I believe this could be possible.

One more thing I'd love to underscore is that Oscar Health expects to serve roughly 3 million paid customers in the second quarter of the year. Now this indicates an above 50% increase on a year-over-year basis. And what that tells me is that OSCR anticipates significantly increasing its market share. That's definitely supporting my bullish thesis. Also, this makes me think that if management can actually achieve such growth, then expectations for the whole year are much more likely to come to fruition. I will be looking forward to monitoring this.

But we won't need to wait long for an update. The company should report Q1 2026 early next month. Now, I will be personally looking at a few things. I'd love to see revenue coming in strong at a double-digit pace. Moreover, I wouldn't be surprised if they posted a drop in SG&A expense ratio. I think the market would like that. Of course, it doesn't mean a positive stock price reaction, but from a helicopter view, that helps its long-term bullish thesis in my honest opinion.

I'd also like it if management provided any AI use cases. Nevertheless, it does seem the stock is likely to benefit a lot from adopting AI in the long run. And surely enough, that's a good thing. What could move the stock significantly, in my opinion, is if they were to increase the guidance even more. I am not sure if that's likely, but that's something to monitor. I'd like to remind you that the stock surged 20% over the past 5 days. Many short-term catalysts may build going into earnings, so I favor a cautious approach.

One Key Trait I Love: Its Defensive Approach

As you may know, healthcare stocks are considered to be defensive. Health is society's priority, so it's not surprising people will be spending money for their well-being and insurance.

It's also not surprising to see multiple AI-related names among the best performing stocks in 2026. That's the trend we have seen over the past few years. But my point is that healthcare stocks may appear to be an interesting way to diversify your portfolio. Historically, if you had healthcare stocks of the S&P 500 trading at a higher multiple, it's more likely a shaky and volatile period for the broader market. And vice versa, when the benchmark is trading at premium multiples, this may indicate that the healthcare sector within the benchmark is suppressed. Now the healthcare names trade at roughly 17x forward P/E, while the S&P 500 is close to 20x. Simply put, this tells me healthcare names may not be the worst pick to diversify into at these levels. And surely enough, that's also bullish for Oscar Health, suggesting the healthcare sector may experience re-rating. And if that happens, OSCR may be positively impacted, too.

I Don't Think Oscar Health Is Expensive

That's true. I don't really think the stock is expensive. OSCR trades at roughly 20x earnings multiple. Of course, one could argue that's more than a 10% premium versus the healthcare peers in the benchmark, which is not cheap by definition. But I'd like to underscore that Oscar Health grew its revenue by almost 28% over the past 12 months. That's close to 3x outperformance versus the average company in the S&P 500 at 10% top-line growth over the past few years. I honestly think that deserves a premium multiple. And a 20x earnings multiple of OSCR matches the forward P/E of the S&P 500 despite the outperformance. So, yes, I don't think it's expensive.

But the valuation may improve significantly. The chart above shows the EPS estimate for 2027. As I pointed out before, management anticipates accelerated growth. If guidance comes to fruition, that makes OSCR roughly 10x forward P/E stock versus the 2027 estimate. It's twice as cheap as the benchmark. I wouldn't be surprised by a re-rating if OSCR continues to deliver strongly.

What I also love about the company is its low leverage capital structure. It has nearly $4 billion in cash and almost $500 million in total obligations. Imagine if OSCR reduces the costs even more; the outlook will improve dramatically in my honest opinion. One more thing I'd like to point out is that OSCR generated $1.09 billion in cash from operations (TTM). Quite frankly, I believe this is impressive. OSCR is only a $4 billion market cap stock, and such cash flow supports my bullish take.

Why My Bullish Thesis Could Not Play Out

There's definitely an outcome where my bullish thesis doesn't play out. Here are the risks I'd love to undermine.

I'd think if the volatility persists for longer, there's a possibility the broader markets could experience another sell-off. Over the short term, I'd expect defensive stocks to be impacted too.

Now, even though it appears that the Middle East conflict is deescalating, no one can be certain. So, if the conflict were to escalate further, that's one reason that could take the markets down. As I stated, OSCR surged by 20% over the past few days, and it's a volatile name. There's surely a risk this appreciation could be sold off if sentiment in the markets weakens.

What I am afraid of is the risk of management underdelivering. I was surprised by their strong guidance, but that's not a guarantee. If the market feels OSCR is not likely to achieve these numbers, sentiment could flip fast. But that's too early to tell. I am looking forward to the next earnings for an update.

Recession is on the table, too. Well, if unemployment spikes, this could affect all stocks, in my honest opinion. One more thing: if inflation rebounds, and let's say the Fed decides to maintain elevated rates for longer, a risk of recession could expand. Also, the risk appetite for small caps could lower due to this. That's not my base case, though.

High Growth Could Ignite Re-Rating

Personally, I like this name a lot. It's a fast-growing insurance company at a relatively insignificant valuation. Obviously, there are risks that could result in suppressed stock performance over the next months. But I like the risk-to-reward here.

It's still a "Buy" stock to me. I don't think the market has really priced in tailwinds. The following earnings could be an inflection point. So, I am looking forward to that. Wall Street analysts forecast a $15.40 price target, presenting 5% upside. It leans toward my bullish take, but I feel a bit more positive about the price action. I wouldn't be surprised if OSCR outperforms the benchmark over the next year. Let's see what the future brings.

data.title: Volaris: Oil Shock And A Volatile Path To Recovery
data.description:
Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación, S.A.B. de C.V. aka "Volaris" (VLRS) stock has gained 12.4% since my last report. In September 2025, I downgraded Volaris shares from Strong Buy to Buy on U.S. market turmoil following increased immigration rhetoric that could lead to a drop in transborder VFR (Visiting Friends and Family) traffic. Generally, I do believe that the company has managed well as it also deals with the GTF engine crisis at RTX (RTX) that keeps parts of its fleet grounded. However, increasing fuel prices provide a challenging background for Volaris.

Volaris Set To Combine With Viva

In December, Volaris and Viva announced that the two companies would merge. The objective goes beyond realizing synergies and into the realm of obtaining a stronger position to negotiate airplane leases and provide the combination with a stronger competitive position. The deal will be financed through the issuance of shares, making Viva shareholders 50% owner of the combined company. So, there is a big change coming up for Volaris shareholders.

Volaris Is Highly Dependent On Oil Price Development

Unsurprisingly, Volaris stock price surged 12.2% in the most recent trading session in a broader market rally following the ceasefire in Iran. However, we note that the stock price is still substantially below the pre-war price setting. My concern with the broader market move up including the rally in oil prices (CO1:COM) is that the ceasefire on which the rally is based reflecting hopes on oil supply to recover is fragile.

The reality is that GDP impact and potential inflation impacts are still there as high oil prices and elevated crack spreads. Airlines are offsetting higher oil prices through increased bag fees and ticket prices. Volaris has a highly price sensitive customer pool. Like many of its peers, the best way for Volaris to increase the revenue per passenger would be through increased bag fees but I believe that ticket price increases are also needed.

For Volaris, the net impact of high oil prices and crack spreads and inflation on the cost side against increased bag fees and ticket prices would be negative 0.4% on revenues and -10% on EBITDAR, while free cash flow would contract 19.1%.

Modeling The Impact On Volaris Share Price

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

In this assessment, I am not factoring in the merger of Volaris with Viva, but instead focusing on the impact of the current dynamics. We do note that there is a lot of uncertainty in the market right now so the situation is highly dynamic. However, in order to provide a conservative view, I am applying the pressured throughout the forecast period.

Volaris has quite a funky projected price target throughout the years and my buy rating is one that is a high-risk one. However, even with a pressure on oil cost and GDP and inflation modelled in with some partial offsets, I do believe that the stock has upside towards 2027 earnings. The peer group EV/EBITDA multiple would suggest 7% upside while the company median EV/EBITDA would suggest 64% upside. My price target is somewhere in between those figures at $10.43 indicating 35% upside in the bullish case and $9.35 in a more conservative setting.

For Volaris, I do expect that the current elevated cost environment will result in EBITDA margins to fall to 8.6% with a slight recovery in 2027. The company should be able to reduce its net debt leverage to healthier levels by 2027. This is not so driven by an improvement in EBITDA, because in our current outlook the EBITDA will decline. However, the company should be able to raise cash through sale-and-lease-back transactions.

Conclusion: Volaris Is A High-Risk Opportunity

I have no doubt that Volaris will face significant pressure from the current cost environment. However, we also note that the company has navigated cost pressures rather well and it is one of the airlines with an extremely strong execution on low-cost. That is also needed as its customer base is cost sensitive. With some increases in bag fees and ticket pricing, I do expect the company to hold its revenues relatively steady with a hit on EBITDA and cash flow generation.

So, it is not the case that I expect a full offset, but I do expect the company to navigate itself to a more manageable position. The combination with Viva Aerobus is one that should drive longer-term strength for the business.

data.title: Central Securities: This CEF Has Quietly Beaten The S&P 500 For Decades; Can It Continue?
data.description:
Not many beat the S&P 500 consistently

I like to collect charts on S&P 500 (SP500) beating funds, pitting them against The State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). As I mentioned in a previous article, when I find one I like to print out the chart and keep it in a binder.

The trend of market beating funds over longer than 10 year periods is that they have a growth stock mandate, of which there are many funds to choose from. These could be funds like the following:

  • Invesco Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ)
  • Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)
  • Vanguard Growth Index Fund ETF (VUG)
  • Fidelity Blue Chip Growth Fund (FBGRX)

All of these growth funds beat the S&P 500 handily over the past decade. The story of the Mag 7 and tech explosion is well known. It may keep going for a decade to come, maybe not. But this excess alpha also came at a price, higher standard deviations, in other words, more volatility.

This standard deviation distribution pattern would tell us that 68.2% [34.1% upside possibility, 34.1% downside possibility] of the time, the fund will be plus or minus up or down 1 standard deviation. For instance if you have a fund with an average compound annual growth rate [CAGR] of 10% and a 10% standard deviation over a set period, the fund would normally be up 20% or return zero percent in any give year 68.2% of the time [10+10=20, 10-10=0].

Then you get to the outliers. 2 standard deviation events are expected to happen 27.2% of the time on either side of the curve. 3 standard deviations are 4.2% of the time and one might not experience such an event in their life time.

Portfolio Analysis Results (Jan 2016 - Mar 2026)

The funds mentioned all have standard deviations over the last decade of near 20%. So we can expect near that level of swings up or down around that mean return in most years. If one is nearing retirement, this is undesirable as you may be selling shares to live on.

My personal preference for a potential fund to buy in lieu of the gold standard S&P 500 funds like Vanguards (VOO), iShares (IVV), or State Streets (SPY) is that they have to meet 3 criteria.

  1. Standard deviation within 150 basis points of SPY.
  2. Better risk adjusted return [Sharpe ratio].
  3. Better 10 year CAGR.

Portfolio Analysis Results (Jan 2016 - Mar 2026) For Central Securities Corp

For Central Securities Corporation (NYSE: CET), this hits every one of my marks.

  • Standard deviation within 150 basis points? - Yes [15.31% vs 15.03% for SPY]
  • Better Sharpe Ratio ? [return over risk free rate divided by standard deviation to measure risk adjusted return]- Yes, .9 vs .8 for SPY
  • Better 10 year CAGR?- Yes, 15.81% vs 13.84% for SPY

Thus in CET, we get a market beating fund that even bested Vanguard's growth ETF VUG with lower volatility.

The story of this closed end fund

The other day I was having a chat with a friend about the succession of management coming in 2026 for the Fidelity Contra Fund (FCNTX). Similar to this fund in the article and ADX, it has beat consistently over the years with equal or better volatility than the S&P 500 proxies. Then we compared it to Peter Lynch and the Magellan Fund (FMAGX), which couldn't beat after Lynch left.

I chalked it up to the complex no-mandate style that Lynch was given. Contra Fund is a large cap active strategy like ADX and simpler to take over, in my opinion. CET seems more similar to what Lynch was doing in the past with the only mandate being to make money and capital appreciation for shareholders, wherever that might be. In that sense, this is a very unique fund especially with this level of volatility which is rather tame.

Fund mandate

The mandate of the fund is very wide in scope. They can basically invest in anything they deem undervalued and that will create long term value for shareholders. Here is an analysis from their site:

The Corporation's investment objective is long-term growth of capital. Income received from investments is a secondary consideration.

In pursuing Central's investment objective, we invest primarily in equity securities that we believe are undervalued at the time of purchase and have the potential for growth.

Central owns a limited number of companies, and it invests for the long-term. Honest management working in the interests of all shareholders is of the utmost importance in the appraisal of investments. We may sell securities for a variety of reasons, including excessive valuation, deteriorating results or to redeploy assets into more promising opportunities.

Central is not restricted as to the types of securities (e.g., equity, fixed income) in which it invests. We may invest in securities of issuers with any market capitalization.

Fund age

This is also a very old closed end fund that has stood the test of time and all sorts of market crashes. Similar to Adam's Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) it has also been around since 1929:

Central Securities Corporation was organized on October 1, 1929, and has operated as a closed-end management investment company since its incorporation. It is the policy of Central Securities to operate as a non-diversified investment company.

Charts over several time periods

In my opinion, 10 year outperformance is a good indicator that current strategies are effective. Since inception total returns can be deceptive as that could be a talented manager up front and the fund not being able to replicate more near term. Let's examine several time periods:

Max chart

5 year

3 Year

As we can see, although the beats become more narrow the more we compress the time frames, that is also a function of compound interest. If something beats consistently, the total return delta will grow wider with longer periods.

The discount

The fund is a closed end fund and can sell at a discount to its net asset value [NAV]. It is currently near the 52 week average at -15.39%.

The holdings

The sectors and types of holdings this closed end fund has are diverse. The big one here is the near 25% private position in The Plymouth Rock Company, a position they have had since 1982. The larger discounts in this closed end fund versus others raise the question of how to value this private insurance holding.

Plymouth Rock is rated below by AM Best:

All of the top 10 positions are at least a decade old. Again, very unique amongst peers.

How about the income?

After running through all the years shown on the CET website for distributions and averaging them out versus the stated reinvestment price as a yield on cost, the average is 7.9% of the market price.

Again, even though the current distribution rate sits at 9.8% according to CEF Connect, I would expect a near 8% yield based on trailing averages over time. This fund doesn't have as strict a mandate of how much capital gains and dividends to pay out as others would, so we use data as our guide.

Risks

This is a very concentrated portfolio coupled with a large concentration of that being a privately held company:

With 32 total holdings, this is one of the more concentrated CEFs you will run into. If Plymouth Rock ever ran into issues, this would be a hard position to get out of with low to no liquidity and a hard-to-determine market value. In essence, consider this both buying an insurance company along with a portfolio of 31 common stocks currently. The very low turnover can be considered a feature or a bug, depending on how much you trust management. I like the 'buy and hold' mentality, but not everyone agrees with this as it relates to mutual, ETF and closed-end funds.

While the fund has successfully managed this strategy and held Plymouth since 1982, it also needs to be watched carefully by investors through rating services.

Summary

I have added this closed end fund to my personal portfolio. For the first time I've started a sleeve of income producing closed end funds which I intend to either use the income from, or strategically reinvest that income as opposed to automatic DRIP in my other accounts. In CET. you have something similar to a Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) with a private insurance position [think Geico], combined with a public portfolio. Main difference here is the income and capital gains get passed down to the investor rather than get reinvested.

Having this private income generator in Plymouth Rock also seems to lead to higher amounts of ordinary income than you would see in typical closed end funds:

Income type Amount in USD %
Ordinary Income 0.85 31.5%
Long-Term Capital Gains 1.85 68.5%
Total Distribution 2.70 100%

Keep this in mind with your asset location as it might be a better holding in tax advantaged accounts for some. I consider CET a buy and a fine fund to add to your collection.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500: This Is Still A Very Weak Market From A Bottom-Up View
data.description:
I wrote an article back on January 21 of this year. That one and this one are really "macro outlook" articles. But with the story told through more of a bottom-up analysis, of the total membership of the S&P 500 index.

That allows me to draw some conclusions about the market as a whole, with some stock-specific examples inside of it to show the broader pattern I see. And that pattern continues to be one of a very weak stock market, with very few pockets of future strength seen.

Financials and some REITS (particularly the digital variety) look generally OK, but that is probably just a clue that rates are likely to dip a bit. All things considered, this is NOT the environment of last year at the single stock level. Not yet anyway.

More than 10 weeks have passed since that previous article I wrote in this same style. And given the breakout of war in the Middle East, the continued penetration of AI into our consciousness, ever-rising inflation concerns, a new presumed Fed chair, and long bond yields levitating again, I figured it was a good time to revisit that research study.

Oh, and by the way: all that has occurred in just the past few months!

The previous article and this one are simply a technical chart scan of the full S&P 500 index. All 500 stocks. I actually do it about once a week, but I really drill down every few months. Or, when market conditions tell me I should. This is such a time.

The S&P 500 is off about 3% since that January 21, 2026 article, following the recent bounce back. So we'll keep that in mind. And with the stock market now more correlated than at any point I can recall in 40 years in the professional investing business, that only added to my intrigue.

First, let's check in on the charts I landed on a few months ago. There were 15 of them. I noted at the time that I was really stretching to find even these. Remember, that's out of 500 stocks, so this list accounted for a mere 3% of the S&P 500 index. I'll also note that the equal weighted S&P 500 ETF (RSP) fell about 3.2% during this time. So just over 1% a month.

Bottom line: this has been a tough slog. And while energy stocks have popped, that's just what they do when 20% of the world's oil supply is in jeopardy of falling under a new set of rules.

This is the original table I presented in that previous article My emphasis is on taking profitable companies, as rated highly by the Seeking Alpha Quant Factor Grade system, then applying my regular chart analysis.

Note that MRNA snuck onto the list despite its low profitability grade. The chart was intriguing enough to include it in that research report.

Here are those 15 stocks, ranked in performance from best to worst over that time (1/21/26-4/8/26). I've also added a column classifying each versus the S&P 500 as a whole.

Fresh Look At All 500 S&P Stocks

Naturally, this is a very fluid situation. The goal here is more to reset the accounting since that last article, and see what a fresh look at all 500 stocks produces.

Given what I see in this table below, the market might be more friendly to short-term macro themes and deeply discounted stocks seeking a fresh look, the list was headed by two real "pops" in the form of a materials stock (CF) and an energy stock (XOM). And right behind them, there's MRNA, up more than 14% in a very mucky market climate.
