The rest range from about +/- 10% except for ERIE at the bottom. There are some consumer staples names in there, and even some REITS, as hope springs eternal for falling interest rates once the war gets off the front page. Though who knows when that will occur.

Which of these still look promising, chart wise?

The underlying pair of themes to this and to nearly every article I'll write here for a while is this:

1. The stock market is as correlated as I've seen it. Ever! It is one risk on/risk off trade. And I don't know if that will ever reverse fully.

2. That being the case, "alpha" in single stock research, while it still has the benefit of making an investor more informed, and promotes "doing things the right way," is frankly losing its usefulness. When a single social post or news event can move nearly all stocks according to whether they are in "this basket" or "that basket," we have a VERY different investing industry.

That's why a stock like CF can rally, then turn on a dime. Not because of anything specific to the stock. Because it is part of a "team of stocks that go up when oil prices do, and the Strait of Hormuz might stay closed." Exxon Mobil (XOM) looks the same.

Hershey (HSY) looks like many stocks in its sector. It has taken a hit, and while it is not yet what I'd call a potential bottoming candidate, its descent is slowing. In a market starved for good stock ideas that can sustain their moves for more than a few weeks, this is something to note any time I see it.

It is essentially saying "not yet, but check back in a couple of weeks, as there might be something important happening, price-wise." Of course earnings season can influence that too. Ditto for Church & Dwight (CHD) here. Carrier (CARR from that list of 15 stocks also fits this description), as does Colgate (CL).

And then there are some stocks from that list which I can place in the category of "false alarms." As in they look lower to me, and so any inkling I had in January of taking a position of size is now past-tense.

KMB's chart is a "modern classic" look to me. It rallied about 12% in just a few weeks. And then, its price momentum just ended. And the PPO indicator show it. It is slip-sliding away.

Which new stocks can I add to this list of "most optimistic charts" within the S&P 500 index?

So, here is an updated set of charts I find most intriguing from within the S&P 500. I skipped the many stocks that look like those consumer staples names I noted above. That is, where they still have more work to do before I can call them "timely" buy candidates.

These look like the most likely to run higher and not just stop on a dime 2-4 weeks later. For that reason, I also leaned on weekly price charts instead of daily. So I could really try to find candidates for more than just a trade. Because trading positions are about all I've found for several months.

All have the solid profitability grades I look for, except for Live Nation (LYV). But in this market, a good chart often trumps a mediocre fundamental picture. And a C grade is not a D or F.

Still we see just how valuation challenged this market continues to be. Nothing higher than a C from this set of 10 stocks that look like the closest among the index's 500 names to be what I call "reasonable risk compared to the upside potential they possess."

There are many stocks I found which are just too early. Translation: they could burst higher by 10% or so, but they'll probably come right back down. And others just look gosh darn awful.

SBA Communications (SBAC) is a good example of how this market doesn't really let you get in until the stock has vacated a good buying zone. SBAC went from "percolating" to up 6% in a day. This is the weekly chart I picked out, and there's room to run. However, as with so many stocks now, the question is how far.

I'll put it this way, there's a decent path to $240. That's up 10% from here. But then what? The stock has stopped climbing at that level four different times since early 2024. That is not a coincidence. It is a symptom of the current market climate.

Netflix (NFLX) is the largest cap stock on this latest list. It quickly rebounded off a 40% drop, and is steadily regaining its footing. There's 30% overhead room to its all time high set last summer. So it is one to watch.

When will I get really excited about the broad stock market again? When stocks like these start turning into winners, on more of an investing time frame than just short-term trades.

Its a $15 billion market cap, so not a stock that will be tossed around with S&P 500 index flows. It is in a currently unpopular part of the technology sector, and sells for 12x trailing earnings. That used to matter to the market. But it hasn't in a while. If and when it does, I'll have a lot more "material" to supply here on single stocks.

Final thoughts for now

The market is still extremely weak here. And while the headlines often make us believe that all we need to do is see the war in Iran calm down, or the Fed lower rates once or twice, or ROI visibility to be seen from the AI spend, I just do not see the type of evidence I need. I've done things a certain way for decades, and there was a time when valuation, earnings and all that were more reliable stock market indicators.

But the index flows and algorithms have taken over. And we'd best get in sync with how they operate, to avoid fighting the proverbial last war.
